bull_thesis: This is a cash takeover, so the bull case is simple: collect the gap to the agreed price. Zijin Gold International has a binding agreement to buy Allied Gold for C$44.00 per share in cash, which converts to roughly US$31.5 on the New York-listed shares. The deal is friendly, the board recommends it, and shareholders have already voted it through by a wide margin with both major proxy-advisory firms (the firms that tell large funds how to vote) backing it. The hardest approval - Canadian clearance under the Investment Canada Act, the one most in doubt given a Chinese state-linked buyer - has already been received, completing Canada. Regional African competition clearances (the West African ECOWAS body and the East/Southern African COMESA body) are also in hand, and the remaining host-country approvals in Mali, Cote d'Ivoire, Ethiopia and Egypt are described as obtained or at advanced stages. With financing secured (Zijin is a large cash-generative miner and the parties amended Allied's credit facility to bridge to closing) and a firm contractual deadline of July 29, 2026, the path is a short list of foreign sign-offs and routine closing steps. If it closes on time, the US shares move from roughly US$23-25 today to about US$31.5 - a wide spread for a deal this far along, driven by the residual foreign-regulatory friction rather than any doubt about the price or the buyer's intent.
bear_thesis: The risk is entirely in the last mile. One extension has already happened - the deadline moved from May 29 to July 29, 2026 - which tells you the foreign host-country approvals are not trivial. If a Mali, Cote d'Ivoire, Ethiopia or Egypt sign-off lags, the parties could slip the date again (it only takes mutual agreement), which would not break the deal but would stretch the timeline and erode the annualized return that justifies holding. A worse tail is geopolitics: a state-linked Chinese acquirer of African gold assets is the kind of transaction that can attract late political friction even after competition clearance, and a broken deal would send the shares back to a standalone gold-producer valuation. The downside cushion is the soft spot in the thesis: the realistic floor is only about US$23, very close to where the US shares already trade, and that floor leans on gold staying strong rather than on any contractual minimum. So while the upside is well-defined, the protection below is thin - a break in a weaker gold tape would hurt.
sop_bull: Event-win value = the cash consideration. C$44.00 per share in cash, which at prevailing exchange rates is approximately US$31.5 per share on the NYSE American line. No business-quality multiple is needed - the buyer pays cash on closing. This is the target.
sop_bear: Deal-break / standalone floor. If the takeover fails, Allied re-rates to a standalone West/East-African gold producer. Given the strong 2026 gold tape and Allied's improved business plan (the same factors that led management to walk away from a cheaper Ambrosia private placement in 2025), the recovery level is roughly the recent unaffected trading price of about US$23 on the New York line - close to where the US shares trade today. This is the verified floor, derived from recent unaffected price rather than a contractual backstop.
sop_breakdown: Probability-weighted blend of the two outcomes. Deal closes at ~US$31.5 (target) with roughly 85% odds given the passed shareholder vote, completed Canadian clearance, regional African competition clearances done, host-country approvals at advanced stages, and a hard July 29, 2026 deadline; deal breaks/slips to the ~US$23 floor with roughly 15% odds reflecting residual foreign-regulatory and geopolitical risk. 0.85 x 31.5 + 0.15 x 23.0 = approximately US$30.2-30.6 per share base-case fair value.
risk_reward: Upside to target ~US$31.5 vs downside to floor ~US$23, off a working live of ~US$25.3: about +6.3 up vs -2.3 down, a roughly 2.8-to-1 reward-to-risk. Off the lower US$23 quote the upside-to-target is wider still (~37%) but the floor cushion is correspondingly thinner.
catalyst_summary: Zijin Gold's C$44.00/share (~US$31.5) cash takeover of Allied Gold - shareholder-approved, Canada and regional African competition cleared, only final host-country regulatory sign-offs remain before the hard July 29, 2026 deadline, with the US shares still ~25-37% below the cash price.
dated_milestone: July 29, 2026 outside date (contractual last day to close); only remaining foreign host-country regulatory approvals plus customary closing conditions stand between here and the cash payout. Extended once from May 29, 2026.
forcing_function: The July 29, 2026 outside date and the receipt of the final outstanding host-country (Mali, Cote d'Ivoire, Ethiopia, Egypt) regulatory approvals - the announcement of those clearances and the closing of the cash arrangement is what forces the US shares to the C$44 cash value.
consensus_delta: The market is treating residual foreign-regulatory risk as far larger than the deal's documented progress warrants. With the shareholder vote passed by a wide margin, both major proxy advisers in favor, Investment Canada Act clearance (the most-doubted approval) already received, and ECOWAS plus COMESA competition clearances obtained, the remaining gate is a short list of host-country sign-offs that management calls obtained or at advanced stages - yet the US-listed shares trade roughly 25-37% below the C$44 cash price. That gap implies a meaningful break probability for a friendly, fully-financed, shareholder-approved deal whose hardest approval is already done. The street is anchored on the one-time extension and the Chinese-acquirer optics, not on the actual approval count.
valley_of_death: The near-term risk is a second deadline slip rather than a cash-burn or maturity wall - Allied is a producing miner with an amended credit facility carrying it to close, so there is no financing cliff. The danger window is the six weeks into July 29, 2026: if a host-country approval lags, the parties can mutually extend again, which would not break the deal but would stretch the holding period and compress the annualized return, and could trigger a brief de-rating in the spread. A sharp pullback in gold during this window would also thin the already-modest downside floor.
positioning_washout: Limited forced-selling dynamics - this is a deal stock held largely by merger-arbitrage and event-driven funds, not a yield-focused or passive base facing a dividend cut or index ejection. The main mechanical risk runs the other way: a confirmed closing would force index and passive funds tracking the name to settle into cash, and any arb funds that trimmed on extension fear would have to chase the spread closed.
moderator_conclusion: This is the funnel working as intended: a hard-dated, binding, shareholder-approved cash takeover with the live price well below the cash payout and a defined (if modest) downside floor. Zijin's C$44.00/share (~US$31.5) cash offer for Allied Gold has cleared the two highest hurdles - the shareholder vote and Investment Canada Act approval - leaving only a short list of foreign host-country sign-offs before the firm July 29, 2026 deadline, yet the US shares sit roughly 25-37% below the cash price for a reward-to-risk near 2.8-to-1. That is an aggressive-entry, defined-risk special situation and I am buying it now rather than capping it like a priced compounder. I hold conviction at 4 rather than a perfect 5 for two honest reasons: the foreign-regulatory gate already forced one extension and could force another, and the downside floor (~US$23, leaning on a strong gold tape) is thin and sits close to today's price - so the asymmetry is real but the cushion is not deep. Value conviction is 3, reflecting that the margin of safety to the verified standalone floor is modest, not generous.
interrogator_dossier:
# Allied Gold Corporation (AAUC) - Event Dossier

**Setup:** Cash takeover (merger arbitrage). This is a special situation, not a bet on the mining business itself. Allied Gold has signed a binding agreement to be bought by Zijin Gold International for C$44.00 per share in cash. The whole thesis is the gap between today's price and that cash payout, and whether the last regulatory door swings open before the deadline.

## 1. The event, in plain terms
Zijin Gold International (the Hong Kong-listed gold arm of China's Zijin Mining) agreed to acquire all of Allied Gold's shares for C$44.00 in cash under a court-approved plan of arrangement (the Canadian legal process for a friendly takeover). The deal is friendly - Allied's board recommends it, and the company's shareholders have already voted it through by a wide margin, with both leading proxy-advisory firms (Institutional Shareholder Services and Glass Lewis, the firms that advise large funds how to vote) recommending a yes vote. What remains is regulatory clearance, not a vote - that is why the catalyst board tags the driver as a foreign-regulator gate.

## 2. Is the catalyst real, dated, and binding?
Yes on all three. Real and binding: a signed arrangement agreement at a fixed C$44.00 cash price, with the shareholder vote already passed; no financing contingency of note - Zijin is a large, cash-generative miner and the parties amended Allied's credit facility to carry the company to closing. Dated: the outside date - the contractual last day to close before either side can walk - was extended once, from May 29 to July 29, 2026 (about six weeks out as of late June 2026). Any further extension needs both sides to agree.

## 3. How credible is the path to closing?
The approval stack is most of the way done. Canada - DONE: Investment Canada Act clearance received, completing the Canadian side; this was the most-watched approval given a Chinese state-linked buyer, and it cleared. Regional African competition - DONE: clearances obtained from ECOWAS (West Africa) and COMESA (East/Southern Africa). Host-country approvals - mostly done: Allied's mines sit in Mali, Cote d'Ivoire, Ethiopia and Egypt, and management describes these host-country approvals as obtained or at advanced stages. A handful of these foreign sign-offs, plus customary closing conditions, are the only remaining gate. The single extension and the careful advanced-stages language are why the market still leaves a wide gap to the cash price.

## 4. The downside floor
If the deal broke, Allied would re-rate back to a standalone gold producer. Gold has run hard in 2026 and Allied's business plan has improved since the deal was struck (the same reasons management walked away from an earlier, cheaper Ambrosia private placement in 2025). The realistic floor is around the recent unaffected trading level, roughly US$23 on the New York line, close to where the US shares trade today. That is a thin cushion, so the defined-risk floor is recovery value as a standalone miner in a high gold-price environment, not a contractual minimum.

## 5. The numbers (US line, NYSE American)
Live price about US$23.0-25.3 (US shares closed near US$23.01 on June 25, 2026; the catalyst board's working live is 25.26). Cash deal value C$44.00, roughly US$31.5 at prevailing exchange rates - the target. Floor about US$23, the recent unaffected/standalone level. The spread from the working live to target is wide for a deal this far along - that width is the asymmetry, and it exists because the last regulatory steps are foreign and not yet fully in hand.

## 6. Red flags / things to watch
Foreign-regulator risk is the whole ballgame - one extension has already happened; a second slip past July 29 is possible if a host-country approval lags (would not break the deal but stretches the timeline and annualized return). Chinese-acquirer optics: although Canada cleared, geopolitics around a state-linked Chinese buyer of African gold assets is a non-zero tail. Thin floor: the cushion below today's price is modest and leans on a strong gold tape holding up.

## 7. Moat / secular - not applicable
This is a defined-risk arbitrage on a cash takeover. Business quality, moat width and secular-decline themes do not carry the thesis; the cash price and the regulatory clock do.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING | MOAT: NONE | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: none
